segments.text: foreign
16 years I got my start back in 2007 as
a junior front-end developer at a
digital asset management company and
back then it was all just build web
pages and then battle to make them work
in ie6
but I didn't last very long there
actually I got the opportunity to join
an agency called mint digital who were
building their applications in Ruby and
rails and so as a front-end developer I
was able to learn the framework and then
Ruby the language
to fully understand the full stack I
used to build sites for TV shows which
is an interesting experience because
you'd launch and then on day one you'd
have a million users but once the TV
show was done those users would just
dwindle away until you could eventually
just close down the application
so then we started building our own
products as well which is the opposite
experience
but my I was I was lucky to lucky enough
to lead a development on a platform
where we use the Instagram API to turn
your Instagram photos into fridge
magnets which was a pleasing success but
whilst it was at mint also I got more
involved in local developer communities
I started attending local events and
hackathons and then speaking at events
and that led me to my next role which
was as a developer evangelist at twilio
and that let me use my experience to
teach developers around the world about
Communications apis about how to send
text messages and make phone calls in
just a few lines of code and then at the
start of this year I joined sonar where
I'm happy to be part of the team
spreading this message of clean code
um you'll find me writing technical
articles or working on open source or
speaking at events around the world to
kind of spread this message and and use
my experience to help developers build
their applications using clean code
principles and ultimately build better
applications
so I was never really a child that knew
what they wanted to be when they grew up
I was I was very indecisive as a kid so
I didn't know I wanted to be a developer
for quite a long time but as I look back
kind of through my life I I can kind of
see a bit of a thread that led me to to
the point where I wanted that first
interactions I had with a computer at
school were were with uh logo you know
the the turtle that you gave
instructions to that would that would
then draw stuff on the screen at the age
of like 13 or 14 I remember doing a
class on HTML I specifically remember
building I think we were building a page
about ourselves and I put things in a
into a table and then gave that table
enormous borders and the background was
made was maroon for some reason that's
absolutely stuck in my head then I I did
do computer science to the end of high
school I think the end of that degree I
was actually uh I didn't think I was
going to be a developer
um that what happened was when I when I
should have been revising for those
final exams I was actually messing
around and building websites for myself
and for other people I knew and
ultimately I realized I could do this as
a job and so JavaScript and HTML and CSS
kind of brought me back into this this
world of being a developer and once I
realized that that's when I kind of
um cracked down to to learn how to do
that and eventually get myself that job
as a junior developer and so I started I
built myself a I built myself a Blog I
started writing about what I was
learning and and that ultimately led me
to to be a developer about a year later
[Music]
so one of the greatest lessons I've
learned in my career as a developer is
that there is far too much stuff in this
industry for any one person to know uh
it's actually the case that people are
pushing the boundaries of development
and of programming every single day
and the more you learn the more you know
there is to learn it's taking a step
back and understanding that you don't
need to learn it all that you don't need
to know it all can melt that stress away
instead it's just important to
understand the fundamentals of the
language and the platform you're
building in and from strong foundations
like that you'll be able to learn
anything else that you need to when you
actually need it
if if you're starting out as a developer
now my advice to you would be to write
about the experiences you have and the
things you learn and preferably publish
that as well there's an enormous number
of benefits to writing about your
experiences when you write about
something as if to explain it to
somebody else you actually have to dig a
little bit deeper into your
understanding of it in order to turn
that into an explanation and it turns
out you end up learning the subject with
greater Clarity and greater
understanding then if you actually
publish this online somewhere those
writings can go towards helping other
people in the community as well the way
you explain something
can could be the key to unlock
understanding for somebody else like my
entire career is based on the fact that
I started writing about web development
and that got me uh foot in the door in
at least the first two odd job
opportunities that I had
um and indeed continuing to write and
learn and write more and publish uh led
me into ultimately
um sharing that knowledge
um in presentations and on stage and
opportunities that have come out of the
fact that I chose to start blogging
about my experiences right at the start
of my career uh have been amazing to me
and I can only recommend uh that you do
the same
as a developer a clean code means to me
writing code like correctly the first
time it means
avoiding writing unnecessary bugs or
overly complicated code or introducing
security issues it's code that you're
confident to deploy to production uh but
happy when you come back to it six
months or a year later to work with
again
a clean code is never Legacy code it's
always just a joy to work with
